sanskrit_text: धृतराष्ट्र उवाच | धर्मक्षेत्रे कुरुक्षेत्रे समवेता युयुत्सवः | मामकाः पाण्डवाश्चैव किमकुर्वत सञ्जय ||१||
transliteration: dhṛtarāṣṭra uvāca | dharma-kṣetre kuru-kṣetre samavetā yuyutsavaḥ | māmakāḥ pāṇḍavāścaiva kimakurvata sañjaya ||1||
meaning: Dhritarashtra said: O Sanjaya, what did my sons and the sons of Pandu do after assembling on the sacred field of Kurukshetra, desiring battle?
explanation: The Bhagavad Gita begins with Dhritarashtra asking Sanjaya about the battlefield of Kurukshetra, setting the stage for the spiritual dialogue between Krishna and Arjuna.

sanskrit_text: कर्मण्येवाधिकारस्ते मा फलेषु कदाचन | मा कर्मफलहेतुर्भूर्मा ते सङ्गोऽस्त्वकर्मणि ||४७||
transliteration: karmaṇy-evādhikāras te mā phaleṣu kadācana | mā karma-phala-hetur bhūr mā te saṅgo 'stv akarmaṇi ||47||
meaning: You have a right to perform your prescribed duties, but you are not entitled to the fruits of your actions.
explanation: This verse teaches Nishkama Karma — performing actions without attachment to outcomes.

sanskrit_text: यद्यदाचरति श्रेष्ठस्तत्तदेवेतरो जनः । स यत्प्रमाणं कुरुते लोकस्तदनुवर्तते ॥२१॥
transliteration: yad yad ācarati śreṣṭhas tat tad evetaro janaḥ | sa yat pramāṇaṁ kurute lokas tad anuvartate ||21||
meaning: Whatever a great person does, others follow. Whatever standards they set, the world follows.
explanation: Leaders influence society through their actions, making righteous conduct important.

sanskrit_text: यदा यदा हि धर्मस्य ग्लानिर्भवति भारत । अभ्युत्थानमधर्मस्य तदात्मानं सृजाम्यहम् ॥७॥
transliteration: yadā yadā hi dharmasya glānir bhavati bhārata | abhyutthānam adharmasya tadātmānaṁ sṛjāmyaham ||7||
meaning: Whenever righteousness declines and unrighteousness rises, I manifest Myself.
explanation: Lord Krishna explains the divine purpose of restoring dharma whenever imbalance arises.

sanskrit_text: ब्रह्मण्याधाय कर्माणि सङ्गं त्यक्त्वा करोति यः । लिप्यते न स पापेन पद्मपत्रमिवाम्भसा ॥१०॥
transliteration: brahmaṇy ādhāya karmāṇi saṅgaṁ tyaktvā karoti yaḥ | lipyate na sa pāpena padma-patram ivāmbhasā ||10||
meaning: One who performs actions without attachment, offering them to the Divine, remains untouched by sin just as a lotus leaf remains untouched by water.
explanation: This verse teaches detached action and spiritual discipline through selfless duty.
